schools.summary: Gill: Paul returns to Lystra where he had been stoned (Acts 14:19), undeterred — zeal and intrepidity; Matthew Henry: Paul a spiritual father adopting Timothy, a wise and tender father; Timothy of a believing Jewish mother (Eunice, 2 Timothy 1:5) and a Greek father, reared in the Scriptures by his mother and grandmother (Lois, 2 Timothy 3:15) — the blessing of godly upbringing; his mixed parentage fitting him for the mixed mission (though raising the circumcision question, v.3).

themes: Timotheus, the son of a Jewess... his father a Greek

schools.summary: Barnes: Timothy esteemed a young man of piety and promise, trained in the Holy Scriptures (2 Timothy 3:15); Gill: witness borne to his piety, virtue, and good conversation; the good report from the brethren a qualification for the ministry (1 Timothy 3:7, 'a good report of them which are without'), character (not mere gifts) the foundation; the testimony widespread (Lystra and Iconium), confirming its truth — Timothy a pattern of the well-reported minister, godly upbringing, good character, and gifts combined.

themes: well reported of by the brethren

schools.summary: Gill: Paul, perceiving Timothy's grace and gifts, desired him as a fellow-labourer and circumcised him 'because of the Jews' to remove offence (not as necessary to salvation); the famous distinction from Titus (Galatians 2:3-5, where Paul refused circumcision because the gospel of free grace was at stake) — with Timothy a matter of expediency (free access to the Jews), with Titus a matter of principle; Paul becoming all things to all men (1 Corinthians 9:20-22), flexible in things indifferent, inflexible where the gospel is concerned.

debates.question: Why did Paul circumcise Timothy but refuse to circumcise Titus?

sides.position: With Timothy a matter of expediency (removing offence, becoming all things to all men); with Titus a matter of principle (the gospel of free grace at stake)

themes: circumcised him because of the Jews
not as necessary but for expediency
becoming all things to all men

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the decrees' (dogmata) the decisions of the Jerusalem council on the four things (Acts 15:20, 29 — things offered to idols, blood, things strangled, fornication), the word meaning a law or edict, here the council's decision; Paul and his company delivering them to the churches to keep — the apostolic settlement of the great question (the Gentiles freed from the law's yoke, requiring only the few things for peace); the churches guided by the common decision, the order and unity of the church maintained.

themes: delivered them the decrees for to keep
ordained of the apostles and elders
the churches guided by the settlement

schools.summary: Barnes: the wise and conciliatory decrees confirmed the churches in the belief of the gospel and increased them; Clarke: the disputes about circumcision had unhinged many, the decrees coming in good time, saving the people from uncertainty; Gill: established both in the grace and doctrine of faith; the double fruit — established in the faith (inward strengthening, steadfast in the truth) and increased in number daily (outward growth); the settled, established church fruitful in growth, the inward health issuing in increase.

themes: the churches established in the faith

schools.summary: Gill describes Phrygia and the route; the Holy Ghost forbade Paul to preach in Asia (the proconsular province with Ephesus) — not because Asia was not to hear (Paul would later labour at Ephesus, Acts 19) but because the time was not yet and God was directing them to Macedonia; the Spirit's sovereign ordering of where and when the gospel goes (by revelation or impulse), the closed door the means of the open one (v.9-10), the apostles submitting to the Spirit's guidance though it crossed their plans.

themes: forbidden of the Holy Ghost to preach in Asia

schools.summary: Gill describes Mysia and Bithynia; the Spirit a second time restraining them (first from Asia, v.6, now from Bithynia) — the divine guidance repeatedly narrowing the way, closing the doors to the side, leaving open only the way to Troas and Macedonia; the apostles 'assayed' (planned/attempted) by their own judgment but submitted to the Spirit's restraint (the servant proposing, the Spirit disposing, Proverbs 16:9); the providence guiding the gospel to Europe by these restraints, the end hidden yet the apostles following.

themes: they assayed to go into Bithynia
but the Spirit suffered them not

schools.summary: Barnes: Troas a city on the Hellespont near the site of ancient Troy, a seaport on the edge of Asia (mentioned in 2 Corinthians 2:12; 2 Timothy 4:13; Acts 20:5); the closing of Asia and Bithynia (v.6-7) bringing them step by step to Troas — the seaport facing Europe, the threshold of the European mission; the providence leading by the restraints to the appointed place where the Macedonian vision would come (v.9) and the gospel cross to Europe, the purpose hidden till Troas.

themes: passing by Mysia came down to Troas
the seaport on the edge of Asia
providence bringing to the appointed place

schools.summary: Barnes: a man known to be of Macedonia (by dress and language) appeared to Paul in a vision (dream or waking, God making his will known both ways), begging 'Come over and help us'; rejects Grotius's notion of a guardian angel of Macedonia; the vision the divine call to carry the gospel across the sea to Europe — the open door revealed after the closing of Asia and Bithynia (the purpose of the restraints made plain); the plea representing the need of Europe (the lost needing the help only the gospel gives), the momentous turn to the West.

themes: a vision... a man of Macedonia
come over into Macedonia, and help us
the open door after the closed ones

schools.summary: Barnes: the first instance Luke refers to himself with Paul ('we endeavoured') — Luke joined Paul and Silas about this time at Troas and attended him thenceforth (the 'we' passages), the historian himself now an eyewitness companion (confirming the narrative's credibility); the apostles, having seen the vision, immediately prepared to go, 'assuredly gathering' the Lord had called them (the vision and restraints together making God's will plain) — the prompt obedience to the divine call, the assurance following the clear direction.

themes: immediately we endeavoured to go into Macedonia
assuredly gathering the Lord had called us

schools.summary: Gill: loosing from Troas (on the Hellespont), the course to Samothracia (an Aegean island) and Neapolis (the seaport of Philippi); Barnes describes Samothracia (peopled from Samos and Thrace); the prompt obedience (immediately setting sail after the call, v.10) and the 'straight course' (direct, favourable, two days) — the providence speeding the passage, the gospel crossing from Asia to Europe (the momentous turn to the West), the favourable voyage contrasting the later harder one (Acts 20:6), Luke's eyewitness 'we' continuing.

themes: loosing from Troas, a straight course

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: Philippi named after Philip of Macedon, famous for the battle of Augustus and Antony against Brutus and Cassius, 'the chief city of that part of Macedonia, and a colony' (a Roman colony with the rights of Roman citizens, a little Rome); the apostles directing their labour to cities of influence (a strategic field); the Roman colony status bearing on later events (Paul's citizenship, v.37); Philippi the first European field (Lydia, the jailer the firstfruits), the gospel's first planting in the West, the providence choosing the field.

themes: Philippi, the chief city... and a colony

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the riverside a customary place of prayer outside the city (the Jews praying not only in synagogues but in set places), on the sabbath when a multitude would gather; marks Paul's freedom from pride, sitting and speaking to the women, one of low condition; finding no synagogue (few Jews at Philippi), the apostles went to the riverside — the European mission beginning in obscurity (a few women at a river), the great work from the small beginning, the gospel for the humble (Lydia, the first European convert, among them).

themes: a river side, where prayer was wont to be made
the humble beginning of the European mission

schools.summary: Barnes: Lydia a seller of purple (a valuable, profitable trade), of Thyatira (famed for dyeing), a worshipper of God (proselyte or God-fearer); 'whose heart the Lord opened' — not Paul's eloquence nor her resolve but the Lord by his Spirit opening her heart to receive the gospel; the necessity of effectual grace (the natural heart closed, 1 Corinthians 2:14, must be opened by God; John 6:44); the means (the preached word, 'heard us') joined to the grace (the Spirit opening the heart); the opened heart attending and believing.

debates.question: What is taught by 'whose heart the Lord opened'?

sides.position: The necessity and sovereignty of effectual grace — God opens the closed heart to receive the gospel (the means the preached word, the power the Spirit)

themes: a seller of purple... which worshipped God

schools.summary: Gill: Lydia baptized in water by the river on her profession; Barnes: baptized without delay, with her household (no mention of their believing), weighing whether this affords presumptive proof of household/infant baptism; the paedobaptist reading (the household, children included, baptized on the head's faith) and the credobaptist (the household believers, baptized on their own profession) — the case cited both ways, the text not conclusive; the substance — the household embraced in the ordinance; Lydia's constraining hospitality the fruit of faith in love.

debates.question: Does Lydia's household baptism support infant baptism?

sides.position: A presumptive proof of household (infant) baptism — the family baptized on the head's faith
The household were believers, baptized on their own profession — no infants stated

themes: when she was baptized, and her household
come into my house, and abide

schools.summary: Gill: 'going to prayer' = to the place of prayer (the oratory), the damsel meeting and following them many days; 'a spirit of divination' a real demonic possession (a 'Python' spirit of soothsaying/fortune-telling), the New Testament recognizing the reality of the demonic (not mere trickery); the girl a slave doubly enslaved (to her masters and the spirit), her possession exploited for her masters' 'much gain' — the greed trading on her bondage and the demonic; the gospel of liberty confronting the powers of darkness.

themes: a damsel possessed with a spirit of divination

schools.summary: Barnes conjectures why she did this — perhaps for gain (to be rewarded), or because the spirit feared expulsion and sought to placate the apostles, or to gain credit by association; the testimony true ('servants of the most high God... the way of salvation') yet from the evil spirit; Paul would not accept it (v.18), for a true word from an evil source is not fit to commend the gospel (as Christ refused the demons' testimony, Mark 1:34; Luke 4:41) — the craft of Satan mingling with the gospel to discredit it, the apostle discerning and refusing.

debates.question: Why did Paul refuse the spirit's true testimony?

sides.position: A true word from an evil source is not fit to commend the gospel (as Christ refused the demons' witness) — Satan's craft mingling with the truth to discredit it

themes: these men are the servants of the most high God
a true word from an evil source

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul 'grieved' = molested, troubled, offended (the testimony troublesome, seeming to ally him with her, from an evil source); Gill: the spirit suffered to continue many days that the expulsion be the more manifest; Paul commanded the spirit 'in the name of Jesus Christ' (not his own power) — the authority of Christ's name over the demonic (Mark 16:17), the apostle the instrument, Christ the power; the spirit came out 'the same hour' (immediate deliverance), the gospel of liberty freeing the doubly-enslaved girl.

themes: I command thee in the name of Jesus Christ
he came out the same hour

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the hope of their gains was gone' — the loss of gain as likely to enrage men as anything; instead of regarding the casting out as a proof of divine power, the masters intent only on their profits, the love of money blinding them to the truths of religion and the proofs of God; they seized Paul and Silas and dragged them to the rulers — greed turned to rage and persecution (1 Timothy 6:10); the irony — the apostles persecuted for the good deed of freeing the girl, the gospel's compassion against the world's avarice.

themes: the hope of their gains was gone
the love of money turned to rage

schools.summary: Barnes: the magistrates the praetors (military rulers, Philippi a Roman colony where army officers exercised civil functions); the masters did not state their real grievance (the lost gain, which would expose their greed) but accused the apostles of being troublesome Jews disturbing the city — the false charge masking the true motive, appealing to anti-Jewish prejudice (the Jews often disliked); the dishonesty of the persecutors (concealing greed under a public-spirited complaint), the innocent charged with the accusers' own crime, the craft of the world against the gospel.

themes: these men, being Jews, trouble our city
masking greed with a prejudiced charge

schools.summary: Barnes: 'customs' (ethe) religious rites or modes of worship, the charge that the apostles introduced a new religion unauthorized by Roman law — 'a cunning and artful accusation' appealing to Roman law and pride ('being Romans'), masking the real grievance (the lost gain) under a charge of illegal religion; Gill: the doctrine of salvation by Christ taken as a new custom; the Romans permitting only authorized religions; the dishonesty of cloaking greed under legality, stirring up national/religious prejudice, the gospel twisted into an illegal cult.

themes: teach customs not lawful for us, being Romans
the gospel charged as illegal religion

schools.summary: Barnes: the beating done in a popular tumult, without even the form of law (no trial, defense, or proof), a violation of the privileges of Roman citizens (Paul later complaining, v.37), contrary to the laws — men affecting zeal for the law while violating it; Gill: the multitude stirred up by the masters and rulers; the magistrates, yielding to the mob, tore off the apostles' garments and ordered them beaten uncondemned — the injustice of the persecution, the apostles scourged for the gospel (2 Corinthians 11:25).

themes: rent off their clothes, and commanded to beat them

schools.summary: Barnes: the Romans unrestricted in the number of stripes (unlike the Jews' forty, 2 Corinthians 11:24), probably inflicting many — Paul's 'in stripes above measure' (2 Corinthians 11:23) referring to this; after the scourging the apostles cast into prison, the jailer charged to keep them safely; the severity of the suffering for the gospel (the servants treated as their Master, the fellowship of his sufferings, Philippians 3:10), the cost of faithful witness (2 Timothy 3:12); the providence overruling — the prison the place of the jailer's coming conversion (vv.27-34).

Barnes: the jailer thrust them into the inner prison (the most retired, secure, protected part, harder to escape) and made their feet fast in the stocks (Greek 'made their feet secure to wood,' the painful confinement); Gill: the jailer doubly careful, having received so strict a charge; the apostles, already scourged, placed in the worst dungeon and racked in the stocks — the suffering compounded; yet in this harsh confinement they prayed and sang at midnight (v.25), the praise rising from the place of pain, the setting for the earthquake and the jailer's conversion (vv.26-34).

themes: thrust them into the inner prison
the setting of the midnight praise

schools.summary: Chrysostom marvels — scourged, wounded, in peril, in the inner prison, fast in the stocks, yet they kept vigil, praying and singing all night; 'what a blessing tribulation is'; the apostles at midnight praying and singing praises (joy and faith rising from the dungeon, not complaint or despair) — rejoicing in suffering (Romans 12:12), the gospel's joy triumphing over pain, the faith that turns the prison into a place of praise; the prisoners hearing them (the witness of joy borne even in the dungeon), the joy the world cannot give nor take.

themes: at midnight Paul and Silas prayed, and sang praises
joy and faith in the dungeon

schools.summary: Gill: an extraordinary, supernatural earthquake (not from natural causes but from the prayers and praises of the saints), God testifying his presence and power; Barnes: in such circumstances a symbol of God's presence and an answer to prayer (as Acts 4:31), designed to furnish proof and lead to the jailer's conversion; the effects — all the doors opened and every one's bands loosed (the supernatural shown in the effects); God answering the apostles' midnight praise (James 5:16), the providence opening the way yet the prisoners not fleeing (the miracle ordered for the conversion to come).

themes: a great earthquake shook the foundations
all the doors opened, bands loosed

schools.summary: Barnes: the jailer supposed the prisoners had fled and that their escape would be charged on him (it being customary to hold a jailer responsible for his prisoners, subject to their punishment, Acts 12:19); suicide approved among the Greeks and Romans when encompassed with danger or disgrace; Gill: awakened by the earthquake; the jailer, a heathen, drawing his sword to kill himself in despair (without the hope of the gospel) — the darkness of the soul without the true God, soon to be turned to faith and joy (vv.30-34), Paul staying his hand (v.28).

themes: the keeper... would have killed himself

schools.summary: Gill: Paul, in the dark dungeon unable to see the jailer, knew by divine revelation what he was about to do (and perhaps God's gracious designs toward him); Barnes: 'Do thyself no harm' the command of religion (enjoining men to do themselves no harm by self-murder, intemperance, lust — Christianity seeking the true welfare of man); Paul's compassionate cry staying the suicide (he the prisoner caring for the jailer's life, returning good for evil), the providence preventing the death that the despairing man might be brought to faith (vv.30-34).

Gill: under Satan's influence (the work of darkness, self-murder) the jailer sought no light, but delivered from the temptation he calls for light (the change wrought, turning from darkness toward light); Barnes: he came trembling, alarmed at the earthquake, amazed the prisoners remained, confounded at the apostles' calmness; the awakening of the jailer (his conscience stirred, the man humbled), falling trembling before the apostles (the keeper at the feet of the kept) — the awakening that precedes faith, the heart prepared, the prelude to his question (v.30).

themes: he called for a light, and sprang in
came trembling, and fell down before Paul and Silas

schools.summary: Barnes: 'never was a more important question asked' — the jailer meant his eternal salvation, not deliverance from temporal danger (the apostles' answer, believe on the Lord Jesus, having no effect on any earthly peril); the cry of the convicted soul, awakened by God's power, seeking salvation; the most important question a man can ask (the concern for the soul, deliverance from sin and wrath); the earnestness of the awakened soul (not idle curiosity but urgent concern), the providence bringing the man through the night's events to the great question.

themes: Sirs, what must I do to be saved?
the cry of the convicted soul

schools.summary: Gill: the faith required not a bare historical faith (mere assent, which may exist without salvation) but a saving faith — looking to Christ alone for life and salvation, relying, trusting, committing the soul to him; the answer 'believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved' — salvation by faith, not works (Ephesians 2:8), the simple, sufficient, free way; 'and thy house' — the same offer to the household (the word preached to all, v.32, the household believing and rejoicing, v.34), the family embraced in the gospel, each to believe.

sides.position: The same gospel offered to the household, who would be saved by believing (the word preached to all, v.32; the household believing, v.34)

themes: believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved

schools.summary: Gill: 'the word of the Lord' the gospel (the word of salvation, reconciliation, peace, life, righteousness, faith — publishing salvation by Christ, by which faith comes); the apostles, having directed the jailer to believe (v.31), preached the word to him and all his house — faith coming by hearing the word (Romans 10:17), the command to believe joined to the preaching; the household embraced ('to all that were in his house'), each to hear and believe; the fullness of the instruction (the gospel expounded, not given in a word only) despite the late hour.

themes: they spake unto him the word of the Lord
to all that were in his house

schools.summary: Barnes: the apostles did not heal their own wounds (restoring others, not themselves); a remarkable instance of religion producing humanity and tenderness — the same man who thrust them into the inner prison now washed their stripes (the cruelty turned to tenderness, faith working by love, Galatians 5:6); 'baptized, he and all his, straightway' — the household baptism (as Lydia's, v.15), debated between paedobaptists (the household on the head's faith) and credobaptists (the household believers, the word preached to all, v.32, rejoicing in faith, v.34); the household embraced in the ordinance.

debates.question: Does the jailer's household baptism support infant baptism?

sides.position: A presumptive proof of household (infant) baptism on the head's faith
The household were believers (the word preached to all, v.32; all rejoicing in faith, v.34), baptized on their own faith

themes: baptized, he and all his, straightway
the fruit of faith in tenderness

schools.summary: Barnes: 'set meat before them' (placed a table, food), the hospitality of the new convert; 'rejoiced' the effect of believing (religion produces joy — free from danger, evidence of forgiveness and friendship with God); 'what a change to be produced in one night!' (the jailer, hours before despairing and ready to kill himself, now rejoicing, believing, hospitable — the transformation complete by grace); the joy of believing (1 Peter 1:8); 'with all his house' — the whole family brought to faith, the marks of a true full conversion (faith, joy, love, the household embraced).

themes: set meat before them, and rejoiced
believing in God with all his house

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the magistrates sent so early ('as soon as it was day') intimating either that they were sensible the midnight earthquake was meant to plead their prisoners' cause, or that their consciences had smitten them; while the persecuted sang in the stocks, the persecutors were full of tossings on their beds (the suffering saints at peace, the unjust judges troubled); the working of conscience (the unrest of the wrongdoers), the providence (perhaps by the earthquake and conscience) moving the magistrates to release the apostles — the reversal of the injustice beginning.

themes: the magistrates sent... let those men go
perhaps moved by the earthquake or conscience

schools.summary: Ellicott: the jailer (now Paul's convert), having spent hours with his teacher, probably learned to use 'go in peace' in the fullness of its meaning (the gospel's peace), not as a mere conventional formula; he naturally looks on the offer (securing safety for his new friend) as one to be accepted; the new convert's gladness at his teacher's release, the love and bond of faith; the convert's simple view (supposing Paul would simply depart) meeting the apostle's larger purpose (v.37, vindicating his citizenship and the gospel).

themes: the magistrates have sent to let you go

schools.summary: Barnes: three aggravating circumstances Paul complains of — beaten contrary to Roman law (forbidding the scourging of a citizen), publicly (so the reparation public), and uncondemned (without trial); to scourge a Roman citizen uncondemned a grave violation; Paul refuses to be thrust out privily, demanding the magistrates come themselves and publicly release them — the lawful use of civil rights to vindicate justice (not revenge but to clear the gospel and apostles and protect the young church); the balance of patient endurance (singing in the stocks) and wise vindication.

themes: beaten us openly uncondemned, being Romans
let them come themselves and fetch us out
the lawful use of civil rights

schools.summary: Barnes: the magistrates feared punishment for imprisoning and scourging Roman citizens in violation of the laws — to punish unjustly a Roman citizen a grave offence, an affront to the majesty of the Roman people, severely punished (the Valerian law: death and confiscation; Claudius depriving the Rhodians of freedom for crucifying Romans); the reversal — the unjust judges, who had beaten and imprisoned the apostles, now seized with fear; the providence turning the tables (vindicating the apostles, protecting the young church from further injustice), the fear that follows wrongdoing.

themes: they feared, when they heard they were Romans

schools.summary: Barnes: a most humiliating act for Roman magistrates, but unavoidable (the apostles had them in their power, could effect their disgrace); probably they besought them by declaring them innocent and pleading ignorance, and desired them to depart (to avoid further tumult and secure themselves from prosecution); the reversal — the proud rulers who ordered the beating now humbly entreating their victims (the proud brought low, Luke 14:11); the public entreaty vindicating the apostles and the gospel (clearing them of crime, protecting the young church).

Barnes: 'comforted them' = exhorted and encouraged them to persevere despite persecution; 'departed' (Paul and Silas) — Luke and Timothy appearing to remain at Philippi (Luke now speaking of Paul and Silas in the third person, the 'we' ceasing until Acts 20:5), some staying to tend the young church; the apostles' first care on release the young church (comforting the brethren at Lydia's, not fleeing in fear) — the pastoral care for the new converts, the encouragement to steadfastness amid persecution, the Philippian church (dear to Paul) founded and established, the firstfruits of Europe.

themes: entered into the house of Lydia
